species.warning: Can be confused with Jack-o'-lantern (toxic) — key difference: chanterelles have forked ridges (not true gills), grow singly from soil (not in clusters from wood), and have a fruity smell.
species.notes: Fruity apricot smell. One of the safest beginner mushrooms when properly identified. Never grows in clusters from wood.

species.warning: DEADLY — responsible for ~90% of mushroom fatality deaths worldwide. Key ID features: white gills, white spore print, skirt (ring) on stem, cup (volva) at base, often partial greenish cap. Symptoms delayed 6-24 hours — by then liver/kidney damage is severe.
species.notes: No antidote. Do NOT taste test. Volva at base is often buried — always dig up the base of any Amanita.

species.warning: DEADLY — all white with white gills, skirt on stem, and cup at base. Often mistaken for edible white button mushrooms. Always check for volva (cup) at base by digging up. White spore print distinguishes it from edible Agaricus (which have pink then brown spores).
species.notes: Contains amatoxins — same deadly compound as Death Cap. No safe dose. Do not eat any all-white mushroom without full identification.

species.warning: Generally safe but avoid specimens growing on yew, locust, or eucalyptus — can absorb toxins from these trees. Some people experience GI reactions, especially when eating older or raw specimens. Cook thoroughly.
species.notes: Very distinctive — no dangerous look-alikes. Chicken-like texture when cooked. Only harvest young, fresh specimens (bright color, soft flesh).

species.warning: Berkeley's Polypore is a look-alike but is also edible. Hen of the Woods tends to return to the same tree base each year.
species.notes: Grows at base of oaks. Can be very large (up to 20kg). Highly prized in Japanese cooking as Maitake.

species.warning: No dangerous look-alikes. Very distinctive appearance.
species.notes: Unique cascading white spines. Seafood/lobster flavor. Also studied for neuroprotective properties. Very easy to identify — beginner-friendly.

species.warning: False Morel (Gyromitra) is a dangerous look-alike. True morels are completely hollow when sliced lengthwise — cap and stem one continuous hollow space. False morels have cottony/chambered insides and a cap that hangs free from the stem.
species.notes: One of the most prized spring mushrooms. Always cook — raw morels can cause GI upset. Hollow interior is the key ID feature.

species.warning: TOXIC — contains gyromitrin which converts to monomethylhydrazine (rocket fuel component). Can be fatal. Key difference from true morel: wrinkled/brain-like cap (not honeycomb pits), cap hangs free from stem, interior is cottony/chambered (not hollow). Some people parboil and eat it — this is risky and not recommended.
species.notes: Slice lengthwise — if not completely hollow, do not eat. Named 'false morel' for good reason. Toxicity varies by specimen.

species.warning: Angel Wings (Pleurocybella porrigens) looks similar but is smaller, pure white, grows on conifer wood, and may be toxic. Oyster mushrooms grow on hardwood and have lilac-grey spore print.
species.notes: Grows in overlapping shelf-like clusters on dead hardwood. Available much of the year including winter. Widely cultivated.

species.warning: TOXIC — causes hallucinations, delirium, nausea, vomiting. The white warts (veil remnants) can wash off in rain, making identification harder. Caesar's Mushroom (Amanita caesarea) is a prized edible that looks similar — orange/yellow cap with orange gills (not white).
species.notes: The iconic red-and-white spotted toadstool. White patches are veil remnants and may wash off. Do not eat.

species.warning: Satan's Bolete (Rubroboletus satanas) is a dangerous look-alike — it has a red/orange pore surface and blues immediately when cut. Bitter Bolete (Tylopilus felleus) is non-toxic but inedibly bitter — check the net pattern on stem and taste a tiny piece (bitter = throw out).
species.notes: Key ID: white/cream pores, net pattern on stem, flesh does NOT blue when cut. One of the world's most prized edible mushrooms.

species.warning: TOXIC — causes severe GI symptoms. Key giveaway: RED pore surface (under cap) and flesh turns vivid blue instantly when cut. Any bolete with red/orange pores should be avoided.
species.notes: Rule of thumb: red pores on a bolete = danger. Also blues intensely when cut.

species.warning: TOXIC — causes severe GI distress. Often mistaken for chanterelles. Key differences: Jack-o'-lanterns grow in clusters from wood/roots (chanterelles grow singly from soil), have true sharp gills (chanterelles have blunt forked ridges), and glow faintly in the dark (bioluminescent).
species.notes: Gills glow faint green in the dark — gives it the name. Always check if growing from wood vs soil.

species.warning: CRITICAL: Always slice puffballs in half before eating. The interior must be pure white all the way through with no sign of gills, stem, or figure inside — if you see any outline of a developing mushroom, it could be a deadly Amanita egg. Common Earthball (Scleroderma) is toxic and has a purple-black interior.
species.notes: Only eat when interior is pure white and firm throughout. Can grow to the size of a football. Easy and safe when correctly identified.

species.warning: TOXIC — looks like a puffball but interior is purple-black (not white). Always slice before eating any puffball. Earthballs have a thick leathery skin and dark interior.
species.notes: The purple-black interior is unmistakable. Slice every puffball before eating.

species.warning: Must be eaten when young and white — they auto-digest into black ink within hours of picking. Do NOT consume with alcohol (or in the days before/after drinking) as some related inkcap species cause disulfiram-like reactions. Cook immediately after picking.
species.notes: Clock is ticking once picked — cook same day. Gills turning pink means it's starting to degrade. Magpie Inkcap (Coprinopsis picacea) is toxic and has black and white pattern.

species.warning: Yellow Stainer (Agaricus xanthodermus) is the main toxic look-alike — it stains bright yellow when scratched at cap edge or stem base, and smells of ink/phenol. Destroying Angel can look similar when young — always check for brown (not white) spore print and pink gills in Agaricus.
species.notes: Brown/chocolate spore print and pink gills are key safe indicators. Scratch the cap base — if it yellows, discard.

flesh_color: bright yellow at stem base when cut

species.warning: TOXIC — causes severe GI upset. Key test: scratch the cap edge or cut the stem base — it stains BRIGHT CHROME YELLOW within seconds. Also has a strong ink/phenol smell (like a hospital). Field Mushroom does not stain yellow.
species.notes: The yellow staining test is reliable and definitive. Always do this check before eating any white Agaricus.

species.warning: MUST be cooked thoroughly — causes GI problems when raw or undercooked. Funeral Bell (Galerina marginata) is a deadly look-alike that grows in similar habitat — key difference: Galerina is much smaller, has a brown spore print (honey fungus is white), and no ring or a thin cobwebby ring. Never eat any honey-colored mushroom growing on wood without absolute certainty.
species.notes: White spore print is critical to check. Must be fully cooked. Funeral Bell has killed people who confused it with Honey Fungus.
